s: The Beat Generation's publishing house, still fighting the good fight
w: Lawrence Ferlinghetti and Peter D. Martin opened this in 1953 as the first all-paperback bookshop in the US. It became legendary when Ferlinghetti was tried for obscenity after publishing Ginsberg's 'Howl' in 1956 — and won. Landmark status, officially. The fight never really stopped.
_facts: Founded 1953 by Lawrence Ferlinghetti and Peter D. Martin
First all-paperback bookshop in the United States
Ferlinghetti tried for obscenity for publishing Allen Ginsberg's 'Howl' (1956) and won
Named an official San Francisco landmark in 2001
Located at 261 Columbus Avenue

s: Jack Kerouac blew off meeting Steinbeck here — chose another drink instead
w: Across Jack Kerouac Alley from City Lights, this 1913 bar is where the Beat Generation drank between arguments. Kerouac famously stood up John Steinbeck for a meeting because he couldn't stop drinking here. Dylan Thomas, Bob Dylan, Allen Ginsberg — all passed through. Some things haven't changed.
_facts: Building designed and built in 1913 by Italian architect Italo Zanolini
Located at 255 Columbus Avenue, across the alley from City Lights Bookstore
Jack Kerouac reportedly failed to meet John Steinbeck because he was drinking at Vesuvio
Core Beat Generation watering hole in the 1950s-60s
Still operating as a bar today

s: First espresso bar west of the Mississippi, open since 1956
w: Giovanni Giotta opened this in 1956, bringing the first espresso machine to the West Coast — it became the unofficial headquarters of the Beat poets and still draws writers, chess players, and the family opera singalongs that happen on Saturday afternoons. Same tiled walls, same cups.
_facts: Opened 1956 by Giovanni Giotta — first espresso-based coffeehouse on the West Coast of the United States
Located at 601 Vallejo Street, North Beach
Considered a San Francisco institution and a local hub for poets, writers, and Beat culture
Known for Saturday afternoon opera singalongs led by the Giotta family

s: The short alley between the bookshop and the bar tells the whole story
w: Formerly Adler Place, this pedestrian lane connects Grant Avenue in Chinatown to Columbus Avenue in North Beach. Renamed for the Beat writer who haunted both City Lights and Vesuvio, it's set with quotations in the pavement — including Kerouac's own words about the felicity of the street. Thirty metres of literary history.
_facts: Formerly known as Adler Alley or Adler Place
Connects Grant Avenue (Chinatown) and Columbus Avenue (North Beach)
Named after Jack Kerouac, a Beat Generation writer who frequented Vesuvio and City Lights
Pavement inlaid with literary quotations including Kerouac's words
Continues across Columbus as William Saroyan Alley

s: A bequest, a beloved eccentric, and the best murals you're not looking at
w: Lillie Hitchcock Coit, who as a girl had been made an honorary member of Knickerbocker Engine Company No. 5, left a third of her estate to beautify San Francisco. The 64-metre concrete column went up in 1933. Inside: 3,700 sq ft of Depression-era WPA murals by 26 artists, often overlooked by visitors heading for the view.
_facts: Built 1932-1933 using Lillie Hitchcock Coit's bequest to beautify San Francisco
Lillie Hitchcock Coit was an honorary member of fire company Knickerbocker Engine No. 5
Tower stands 210 feet (64 m) on Telegraph Hill
Interior contains Depression-era WPA murals by 26 artists covering 3,700 sq ft
Added to the National Register of Historic Places in 2008

s: One block of rotating political murals, repainted as the world changes
w: Run by the Clarion Alley Mural Project since 1992, this single block between Mission and Valencia has been a live canvas for three decades — murals replaced, debated, and updated as issues shift. Never decorative, always urgent. More like a moving editorial than an art walk.
_facts: Runs between Mission and Valencia Streets between 17th and 18th Streets, Mission District
Clarion Alley Mural Project (CAMP) founded 1992
CAMP uses public art as a vehicle for social, economic, racial, and environmental justice messaging
Murals are regularly replaced and updated to reflect current events
One of the most densely muraled streets in the city

s: The most densely muraled block in SF, begun in solidarity with Central America
w: Artists began painting this one-block alley in 1973, and in 1984 a wave of murals went up in response to US policy in Central America. Since then almost every wall surface has been covered. Running between 24th Street and Garfield Square, it's the Mission's open-air archive — political, personal, and perpetually evolving.
_facts: Located in the Inner Mission District between 24th Street and Garfield Square (Calle 24)
Most concentrated collection of murals in the city of San Francisco
Mural painting began here in 1973
1984 wave of murals directly responded to US policy in Central America
Nearly every building surface has been decorated

s: The only public burial place left in SF, hidden in a residential street
w: San Francisco banned new cemeteries in 1900 and spent the following decades evicting the dead — 150,000 bodies were moved to Colma. The Columbarium escaped: built in 1898, its copper-domed neoclassical rotunda sits on Loraine Court, unreachable by Muni, and holds niches you can still buy. Intact, improbable, and quietly beautiful.
_facts: Built in 1898 by architect Bernard J.S. Cahill
Copper-domed neoclassical architecture
Located at One Loraine Court, Inner Richmond
Only non-denominational public burial place within San Francisco's city limits still offering space
San Francisco banned new cemeteries in 1900 and relocated 150,000 bodies to Colma

s: The world's largest indoor swimming complex, now a tidal ruin by the Pacific
w: Adolph Sutro opened this in 1896 — six saltwater pools plus one freshwater, a museum and a skating rink under a glass roof, capacity 10,000 bathers. Too enormous to heat or maintain, it became an ice rink, then a demolition site. A fire in 1966 finished it. The concrete pools still fill and drain with the tides.
_facts: Opened 1896 by Adolph Sutro as a large privately owned public saltwater swimming pool complex
Contained six saltwater pools and one freshwater pool under a glass roof
Could accommodate 10,000 visitors at once with museum, skating rink and concessions
Closed and began demolition in 1966; a fire the same year destroyed what remained
Located in the Lands End area, Outer Richmond District

s: The only Victorian-era house in SF you can actually go inside
w: Built in 1886 for merchant William Haas, this Queen Anne pile survived the 1906 earthquake and fire while most of the neighbourhood burned around it. In 1973 it passed to San Francisco Heritage as a museum — original wallpapers, original furnishings, still arranged as if the Lilienthals just stepped out.
_facts: Built in 1886 for William and Bertha Haas at 2007 Franklin Street
Survived the 1906 San Francisco earthquake and subsequent fire
Listed on the U.S. National Register of Historic Places and a San Francisco Designated Landmark
Donated to nonprofit San Francisco Heritage in 1973 and converted to a museum
Only Victorian-era house in SF open to the public as a period museum

s: The needle that became the city's silhouette despite everyone hating it first
w: William Pereira's 48-storey pyramid at 600 Montgomery was denounced as ridiculous when plans were revealed in 1969 — a developer's folly, too spiky, un-San Franciscan. Completed in 1972, it was the world's eighth-tallest building. The company it was named for later left. The pyramid stayed and somehow became the city itself.
_facts: Designed by architect William Pereira, completed 1972
Stands 853 feet (260 m), 48 storeys, at 600 Montgomery Street
Was the tallest building in San Francisco from 1972 until 2017 (surpassed by Salesforce Tower)
On completion in 1972 it was the eighth-tallest building in the world
Transamerica Corporation later moved its headquarters to Baltimore, but the building remains the company's logo

s: Coppola's copper-green flatiron, still housing his film studio today
w: This 1907 copper-clad flatiron survived the earthquake and became a bar, then a pawnshop, then a strip club. Coppola bought it in 1972 with Godfather profits to house American Zoetrope — the distinctive verdigris wedge at Columbus and Kearny, SF Designated Landmark No. 33, is still his studio's home today.
_facts: Completed 1907, survived the 1906 earthquake (still under construction at the time)
Distinctive copper-green Flatiron style structure at Columbus Avenue, Kearny Street, and Jackson Street
Francis Ford Coppola purchased the building in 1972 using profits from The Godfather
Houses American Zoetrope film studio on most floors
San Francisco Designated Landmark No. 33

s: Home of the world's rudest waiter, a Chinatown institution since 1912
w: From 1912 the original Sam Wo on Washington Street ran on three floors with a dumbwaiter, passing through the kitchen on your way to your table. In the 1960s Edsel Ford Fung — 'the world's rudest waiter' — made it infamous by insulting every customer. Closed in 2012, reopened on Clay Street in 2015. Still there.
_facts: Restaurant founded 1912 at 813 Washington Street, Chinatown
Edsel Ford Fung worked there in the 1960s and was known locally as 'the world's rudest waiter'
Original location had three floors connected by a dumbwaiter, with access through the kitchen
Closed in 2012 due to health and safety concerns
Reopened in 2015 at nearby 713 Clay Street and still operates

s: California's oldest restaurant, serving charcoal-broiled fish since 1849
w: Three Croatian immigrants set up a coffee stand on the docks during the Gold Rush; it became a restaurant in 1849 and has never closed. The broiled seafood comes off the same charcoal-and-mesquite setup that's been running for generations. Curtained wooden booths, no reservations, always a queue at 240 California Street.
_facts: Founded 1849, making it the oldest continuously operating restaurant in California
Located at 240 California Street in the Financial District
Founded by Croatian immigrants who set up a coffee stand during the Gold Rush
Features Croatian-style cooking techniques: grilling seafood over mesquite and charcoal broilers
No reservations policy; seats in curtained wooden booths

n: Mechanics' Institute Library and Chess Room
s: A Gold Rush library with one of the oldest chess clubs in the country
w: Founded in 1854 to serve jobless miners who needed skills and company, the Mechanics' Institute at 57 Post Street has been a private membership library ever since — one of the oldest on the West Coast. The chess room on the upper floor has hosted grandmaster tournaments since the 1880s. Quiet up there, dusty, and serious.
_facts: Founded in 1854 as a mechanics' institute to serve out-of-work gold miners
Located at 57 Post Street, Financial District
One of the oldest membership libraries on the West Coast
Chess club has been active since the 1880s, hosting grandmaster-level tournaments
Still operates as a private membership library, cultural event centre, and chess club

s: Tiny alley factory making fortune cookies by hand since the 1960s
w: Tucked into Ross Alley — Chinatown's oldest surviving alley — this tiny factory has been pressing fortune cookies on iron griddles and folding them by hand for decades. You watch through a window: a woman, a stack of blanks, and a rhythm. The fortunes are folded in while the discs are still warm. Also sells fortune-cookieless variants.
_facts: Located at 56 Ross Alley in Chinatown, one of San Francisco's oldest alleys
Produces fortune cookies by hand using traditional iron griddle presses
A family-run operation open to visitors who can watch the production process
Fortune cookies folded by hand while still warm from the griddle
Also sells 'naughty' fortune variants and plain cookies without fortunes

s: Taxidermy, carnivorous plants, and neo-Victoriana on Valencia Street
w: Founded 1992 by Sean Quigley, Paxton Gate defies description: part natural history museum, part steampunk gardening store, part taxidermist. Carnivorous plants crowd the shelves alongside mounted insects and antique scientific instruments. Named after British gardener-architect Sir Joseph Paxton. Go in, linger.
_facts: Founded 1992 by Sean Quigley at 824 Valencia Street, Mission District
Named after British gardener and architect Sir Joseph Paxton
Stocks taxidermy, mounted insects, carnivorous plants, succulents, and steampunk-adjacent oddities
Described as 'a natural history museum merged with a home products store and quirky gardening shop'
Offers workshops including insect pinning and taxidermy

s: SF's oldest building, with a graveyard Hitchcock used for 'Vertigo'
w: Founded by Franciscan missionaries in 1776, the adobe chapel completed in 1791 is the oldest surviving structure in San Francisco. The tiny graveyard holds early settlers, native Ohlone people, and the character Carlotta Valdes — Hitchcock used the cemetery as a location in 'Vertigo' (1958). The basilica next door came 127 years later.
_facts: Founded 1776 by Spanish Franciscan missionaries; formal name Mission San Francisco de Asis
Adobe chapel completed 1791 — the oldest intact structure in San Francisco
Graveyard contains early settlers and members of the indigenous Ohlone people
Alfred Hitchcock used the cemetery as a filming location for 'Vertigo' (1958)
Mission Dolores Basilica built 1918, designated a minor basilica by Pope Pius XII in 1952

s: A working 1903 windmill that once pumped all of Golden Gate Park's water
w: Built in 1903 to pump groundwater and irrigate Golden Gate Park — at peak it moved 1.5 million gallons a day. The vanes stopped turning in the 1930s when electric pumps arrived. Restored in 2012, it stands at the park's western edge beside the Queen Wilhelmina Tulip Garden. Landmark status since 1981.
_facts: Completed 1903 on the northern edge of Golden Gate Park at the western end
Built to pump groundwater for irrigating Golden Gate Park, capable of moving 1.5 million gallons per day
Ceased pumping in the 1930s when replaced by electric pumps
Designated a San Francisco Landmark on December 6, 1981
Restored in 2012; stands next to the Queen Wilhelmina Tulip Garden

s: The cathedral of used records, holding the line on physical media since 1990
w: When Amoeba opened at 1855 Haight Street in 1997 — the Berkeley original launched in 1990 — it brought 100,000+ records and CDs into a former bowling alley. Vinyl, cassettes, DVDs, zines. The staff picks are eerily good. Somehow survived the streaming era; somehow still packed most days. The Haight's secular temple.
_facts: Amoeba Music founded 1990 in Berkeley; San Francisco location at 1855 Haight Street
Housed in a former bowling alley building in Haight-Ashbury
Stocks over 100,000 used and new vinyl records, CDs, cassettes, and other media
One of the last major independent music stores in the US, survived the CD and streaming declines
Focuses on vinyl and compact discs alongside rock, pop, blues, soul, funk, jazz, and rap

s: SF's oldest surviving cinema, non-profit and still showing the rare stuff
w: Founded in 1912 at 3117 16th Street, the Roxie is the oldest movie theatre in San Francisco still in operation. It survived the multiplex era by going non-profit and leaning into repertory and documentary programming. No advertising, no corporate concessions, queues of regulars on the pavement. The Mission's arthouse anchor.
_facts: Founded 1912 at 3117 16th Street, Mission District
Oldest continuously operating movie theatre in San Francisco
Operates as a non-profit community arthouse cinema
Specialises in repertory, documentary, and independent film programming
Located at 3117-3125 16th Street, near Valencia Street

s: Where the Dead lived during the Summer of Love, now just a tidy Victorian
w: In 1967 the Grateful Dead rented 710 Ashbury — Jerry Garcia, Bob Weir and the rest living communally here during the Summer of Love. Janis Joplin was around the corner. Police raided the house in October 1967 and arrested several band members. Now a private residence with a small fan-maintained plaque.
_facts: The Grateful Dead rented 710 Ashbury Street from 1966 to 1968
Band members including Jerry Garcia and Bob Weir lived communally at this address during the Summer of Love (1967)
Janis Joplin lived a few blocks away at 635 Ashbury Street
Police raided the house on October 2, 1967, arresting several band members for marijuana possession
Now a private residence; small plaque marks the building's history

s: An art deco supper club from 1931, still booking live acts at 1025 Columbus
w: The building started as the Bal Tabarin nightclub in 1931 and merged with a separate 365 Club in 1951 under Agostino 'Bimbo' Giuntoli. The interior — drapes, booths, a proper stage — has barely changed. Still a venue, still the same room where Sinatra played. One of SF's oldest live rooms.
_facts: Building originally opened as Bal Tabarin in 1931 at 1025 Columbus Avenue
The separate '365 Club' at 365 Market Street merged with this location in 1951 under owner Agostino 'Bimbo' Giuntoli
One of San Francisco's oldest surviving nightclub sites
Specialises in live rock and jazz shows
Interior largely unchanged from mid-20th century, with original drapery, booths, and stage

s: The cafe that introduced Irish coffee to America in 1952
w: Travel writer Stanton Delaplane smuggled the Irish coffee recipe back from Shannon Airport in 1952 and worked with barman Jack Koeppler to perfect it at the Buena Vista. They went through a thousand attempts before nailing the cream float. The cafe, opened as a saloon in 1916, now serves up to 2,000 Irish coffees on a busy day.
_facts: The Buena Vista cafe opened originally in 1916 as a boardinghouse saloon at 2765 Hyde Street
Credited with introducing Irish coffee to the United States in 1952
Travel writer Stanton Delaplane brought the recipe from Shannon Airport, Ireland
Barman Jack Koeppler and Delaplane spent considerable effort perfecting the cream float technique
Now serves as many as 2,000 Irish coffees per day on busy days

s: The Castro's town square, anchored by the flag Milk helped put there
w: At the corner of Market, Castro, and 17th Streets, this plaza marks the southern entrance to Castro station — and the neighbourhood Milk made his base when he opened a camera shop here in 1972. After his assassination in 1978 the community raised a giant rainbow flag. A permanent flag now flies from a 40-metre pole.
_facts: Located at the intersection of Market Street, Castro Street, and 17th Street
Serves as the southern entrance to Castro station (Muni Metro)
Harvey Milk opened a camera shop at 575 Castro Street in 1972, making the neighbourhood his political base
Harvey Milk was assassinated on November 27, 1978
A large rainbow flag flies permanently from a tall flagpole at the plaza in his memory

s: Two long concrete slides cut into a Castro hillside, open to everyone
w: Hidden up Acme Alley off Seward Street, two concrete slides drop about 30 metres down a steep grassy hillside — built by the city's Recreation and Parks department and utterly free. You bring your own cardboard for speed. Parents, skaters, adults testing how much of their dignity has survived — the slides don't discriminate.
_facts: Located on Seward Street at Acme Alley in the Castro District
Two concrete slides built into a steep hillside in a city park
Free to use, maintained by San Francisco Recreation and Parks
Riders traditionally bring cardboard to increase speed
Open to all ages; one of the city's hidden recreation spots

s: Named for the Beat poet who kept a 12-year vow of silence in San Francisco
w: Bob Kaufman arrived in SF in the late 1950s and became a founding figure of the Beat movement — more famous in France, where he was called the 'Black American Rimbaud.' On the day Kennedy was shot he took a vow of silence, said nothing for twelve years, then walked into a coffee shop and recited a poem. This alley carries his name.
_facts: Named after Robert Garnell Kaufman (1925-1986), an American Beat poet and surrealist
Kaufman was known in France as 'the Black American Rimbaud' where his poetry had a major following
Kaufman took a vow of silence on the day of Kennedy's assassination (November 22, 1963)
He broke the silence in 1973 by walking into a San Francisco coffee shop and reciting a poem
Alley is located in North Beach, the heart of the Beat Generation scene
